coredata.dc:title: Investigation and risk assessment of dibutyl phthalate in a typical region by a high-throughput dual-signal immunoassay
coredata.dc:creator: Xiong, Dinghui
Zhu, Nuanfei
Zhu, Fang
Yakubu, Salome
Lv, Jungang
Liu, Jingfu
Zhang, Zhen
coredata.dc:description: The systematic investigation and risk assessment of dibutyl phthalate (DBP) were performed using an ultrasensitive dual-signal immunoassay in Zhenjiang, Jiangsu Province. In this study, C-dots@H-MnO2 nanohybrid were synthesized and labelled on the secondary antibody to generate fluorometric and colorimetric signals. Attributed to the efficient catalysis of carbon dots (C-dots) and the high C-dots loading of hollow manganese (IV) oxide (H-MnO2), the excellent sensitivity and low detection limits (0.243 and 0.692 μg/L respectively) were produced. Based on the proposed method, 25 water and 119 beverage samples were investigated. DBP was found in all water samples and 65.5% of beverage samples, with the concentrations varying in 16.5–32.1 μg/L and 0–553 μg/L, respectively. In addition, the mean concentration (22.9 μg/L) in waters was decreased significantly compared with that detected in 2016 (43.5 μg/L) by our Lab. For beverages, a similar phenomenon was observed by the measured concentrations from coffee. Furthermore, the potential ecological risks of DBP were evaluated, the results indicated that human activities had caused serious pollution and high risks to the local aquatic ecosystem. On the other hand, the results of health risk assessment suggested that DBP in beverages might not cause obvious side effects to local residents.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The systematic investigation and risk assessment of dibutyl phthalate (DBP) were performed using an ultrasensitive dual-signal immunoassay in Zhenjiang, Jiangsu Province. In this study, C-dots@H-MnO2 nanohybrid were synthesized and labelled on the secondary antibody to generate fluorometric and colorimetric signals. Attributed to the efficient catalysis of carbon dots (C-dots) and the high C-dots loading of hollow manganese (IV) oxide (H-MnO2), the excellent sensitivity and low detection limits (0.243 and 0.692 μg/L respectively) were produced. Based on the proposed method, 25 water and 119 beverage samples were investigated. DBP was found in all water samples and 65.5% of beverage samples, with the concentrations varying in 16.5–32.1 μg/L and 0–553 μg/L, respectively. In addition, the mean concentration (22.9 μg/L) in waters was decreased significantly compared with that detected in 2016 (43.5 μg/L) by our Lab. For beverages, a similar phenomenon was observed by the measured concentrations from coffee. Furthermore, the potential ecological risks of DBP were evaluated, the results indicated that human activities had caused serious pollution and high risks to the local aquatic ecosystem. On the other hand, the results of health risk assessment suggested that DBP in beverages might not cause obvious side effects to local residents.

ce:section.ce:para: Dibutyl phthalate (DBP), one of the most common phthalate acid esters, has been widely used in plastic manufacturing as plasticizers to increase softness, flexibility, and durability of the polymer (Ma et al., 2020). However, due to its noncovalent bound with plastic polymer, DBP tends to leach out during use or disposal (Zhang et al., 2020), ultimately rendering the ubiquity of DBP in various environmental matrices and foods, including water bodies (Ai et al., 2021), soils (Feng et al., 2021), air dust (Anh et al., 2021), vegetables (Y. Zhang et al., 2019), packaged foods (Sakhi et al., 2014), etc.

DBP can enter organisms via different pathways such as ingestion and breathing, and enrich through the food chain, which may eventually threaten the safety of ecosystem and human health (Staples et al., 1997). As an endocrine-disrupting chemical, numerous studies had confirmed the ecotoxicity of DBP to aquatic organisms (Chen et al., 2014; Mo et al., 2019). Moreover, some studies suggested that DBP was associated with the harmful effects on male reproduction and development (Kavlock et al., 2002; van den Driesche et al., 2015). Considering its potential biotoxicity and extensive applications, adverse effects of DBP on environment and organisms should be examined (Sha et al., 2007). Risk assessment based on the results of its exposure is an effective way to quantify the threats of DBP (Zhang et al., 2020a). Some researchers analyzed the level of DBP exposure via diets and assessed its potential health risk (Wang et al., 2021; Sirot et al., 2021), others focused on the ecological risk of DBP in diverse environmental matrices (Zhang et al., 2021; Liu et al., 2020). However, health risk and ecological risk assessments are typically carried out separately which may bring about misjudgments of the contaminant threats. In fact, harmlessness to humans does not inevitably imply safety to ecosystems (Suter Ii et al., 2003). Some aquatic organisms may be more susceptible to DBP exposure through surface runoff, sewage discharge, and atmospheric deposition (Mo et al., 2019; Li et al., 2017). As such, it is necessary to systematically assess DBP threats, including health risks in foodstuffs (as dietary intake dominated the source of exposure to DBP) (Wormuth et al., 2006; Abtahi et al., 2019) and ecological risks in aquatic environments.

At present, instrumental analytical techniques are considered to be the most commonly used methods for the exposure levels of DBP investigation, such as liquid chromatography tandem mass spectrometry (LC-MS/MS) (N. Li et al., 2021; Shin et al., 2020) and gas chromatography mass spectrometry (GC-MS) (Anh et al., 2021; Zhang et al., 2019). However, the drawbacks of these methods (being expensive, time-consuming and low-throughput) limited their widespread applications in the risk assessments of pollutants. With these methods, some researchers spent enormous amounts of time and energy on analyzing samples (Chang et al., 2014; Ding et al., 2019), otherwise, insufficient amounts of samples may lead to incomprehensive investigations (Sakhi et al., 2014). In contrast, immunoassay is a low-cost, convenient and effective method, which could be used to rapidly detect organic contaminants (Liang et al., 2017). Given that conventional enzyme linked immunosorbent assay (ELISA) cannot meet the requirements of trace pollutants analysis due to its poor sensitivity and accuracy (Fu et al., 2017), an improved ELISA with high sensitivity and accuracy is urgently needed to investigate the occurrence of DBP and its further risk assessment.

A variety of ELISAs have been developed for detecting DBP with satisfactory sensitivity (Zhang et al., 2018; Zhu et al., 2021). Unfortunately, most results of these are determined based on the single-signal readout, which typically suffer from false positives in complex samples (Zhou et al., 2021). In this work, the dual-signal sensing was employed to provide different signals for mutual-verification, thus greatly minimizing false results (J.W. Li et al., 2021; Pruksaphon et al., 2021). The hollow manganese (IV) oxide (H-MnO2) with large cavities was synthesized for loading high quantities of carbon dots (C-dots). Unlike traditional fluorescent materials, the C-dots not only have prominent photostability and brightness (Zhou et al., 2020), but also can act as a photo-oxidase nanozyme to promote the oxidization of 3,3′,5,5′-tetramethylbenzidine (TMB) to induce an intense blue color (J. Zhang et al., 2019). Attributed to the precisely controlled release of C-dots and the efficient catalysis of C-dots/Mn (II) system, the quick-response colorimetric and fluorescence signals were produced.

The major objectives of this study are (I) to develop a colorimetric/fluorometric dual-signal ELISA for rapid, accurate and sensitive detection of DBP; (II) to systematically investigate DBP occurrence in waters and beverages; (III) to explore the changes in the pollution status of DBP by comparing the data we measured in 2016; (IV) to conduct ecological risk assessment and health risk assessment of DBP. The assessment results will provide theoretical basis for the effective control of DBP at the typical region.

Cyclohexane, hexanol, Triton X-100 (TX-100), and tetraethyl orthosilicate (TEOS) were purchased from Alfa Aesar Chemical Ltd. (Tianjin, China). Citric acid, ammonium hydroxide, ethylenediamine, (3-aminopropyl)-triethoxysilane (APTES), KMnO4, Na2CO3, H2O2 and 3,3′,5,5′-tetramethylbenzidine (TMB) were obtained from Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Co. Ltd. (Shanghai, China). 1-(3-Dimethylaminopropyl)− 3-ethylcarbodiimide hydrochloride (EDC), polyethyleneimine (PEI), and goat anti-rabbit IgG were provided by Sigma-Aldrich (USA). All chemicals were of analytical grade unless otherwise specified. The polyclonal antibodies against DBP were produced in our lab, and the cross-reactivities toward structurally related compounds of DBP were shown in Table S1.

Buffers and solutions: The coating buffer solution (CBS) was prepared by dissolving 0.80 g of Na2CO3 and 1.47 g of NaHCO3 in 500 mL of ultra-pure water; blocking buffer was CBS solution which contained 1% gelatin; PBST was phosphate-buffered saline (PBS) containing 0.05% Tween 20.

The transmission electron microscopy (TEM) analysis was done with a JEM-2100 TEM instrument (JEOL, Japan) at an accelerating voltage of 200 KV. The field emission scanning electron microscopy (FESEM) imaging, color mapping and energy dispersive spectroscopy (EDS) analyses were performed using the FESEM instrument (JSM-7800F, JEOL, Japan) with a separate EDS detector (JSM-2100F, JEOL, Japan) connected to that instrument. The FESEM instrument has magnification 25X to 1000 KX and works at the accelerating voltage of 15 kV. The X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) analysis was carried out using a Kratos Axis-Ultra DLD instrument with a monochromatized Al Kα radiation (1486.69 eV) and with a concentric hemispherical analyzer working at 15 kV and 10 mA. The pressure of sample analysis chamber was lower than 3 × 10−9 Torr. The X-ray beam is normal to the sample and the detector is 45° away from the incident direction. X-ray diffraction (XRD) patterns were obtained from a diffractometer (D8 ADVANCE, Bruker, Germany) with a scan rate of 5° min−1 in the 2θ range from 5° to 80°. The particle size and surface zeta potential were determined using the zetasizer analyzer (Nano ZS90, Malvern, UK). Additionally, the fluorescence spectra were recorded with a microplate reader (Infinite M1000 Pro, TECAN, Switzerland), the UV-Vis absorption spectra were obtained using the UV-Vis spectrophotometer (UV-2600, SHIMADZU, Japan) equipped with a 1 cm quartz cuvette holder for liquid samples.

For SEM, EDS, XPS and XRD analyses, the prepared powder materials were directly used for the measurements. The sample for TEM was prepared by droping the suspension of materials onto a carbon-coated Cu grid followed by drying at ambient conditions. For other characterizations, the suspension of materials prepared by diluting and sonicating the dried samples was directly used.

The C-dots were prepared via hydrothermal polymerization of citric acid and ethylenediamine as described in previous studies (J. Zhang et al., 2019). Briefly, citric acid (1.0 g) and ethylenediamine (335 μL) were dissolved in ultra-pure water (5 mL) under ultrasonication for 2 min. The mixture then was transferred to a Teflon-lined autoclave, and kept in an oven at 240 °C for 5 h. After cooling down to room temperature, C-dots were further purified by dialysis. Finally, the dark brown C-dot powders were obtained by freeze-drying.

H-MnO2 was synthesized following the reported protocol (Yang et al., 2017; Pan et al., 2019). A mixture of TX-100 (5.3 mL), cyclohexane (22.5 mL), and n-hexanol (5.4 mL) was added into a 100 mL flask and stirred for 5 min, followed by 0.75 mL ammonia (28 wt%) and 1.0 mL deionized water, and then continue to stir for 30 min. Afterwards, TEOS (500 μL) and APTES (100 μL) were added to the mixed solution, which was kept reacting for 24 h at room temperature to get SiO2 nanoparticles after a step of centrifugation (12,000 rpm, 10 min) and washing. Under ultrasonication, KMnO4 (150 mg) was added into the suspension of SiO2 (200 mg), through 6 h reaction and the subsequent procedures of centrifugation and washing, the SiO2@MnO2 was produced. Then the hard template (SiO2) was dissolved by mixing SiO2@MnO2 nanoparticles with 2 mol/L Na2CO3 (200 mL) at 60 °C for 12 h (Doustkhah et al., 2021a), and the mesoporous H-MnO2 was collected by centrifugation. Finally, C-dots (5.0 mg) was added to the solution containing H-MnO2 and stirred for 24 h in darkness to form C-dots@H-MnO2.

The C-dots@H-MnO2 nanohybrid was labelled on Ab2 through the covalent binding between carboxyl and amino groups (Xiao et al., 2020). Briefly, the C-dots@H-MnO2 nanohybrid was mixed with PEI (100 mg) in 20 mL ultrapure water under 30 min of ultrasound, subsequently EDC (200 mg) was added to the mixture. After 8 h of stirring, the products were washed with water several times and dispersed in PBS (10 mM, pH 7.4 with 0.1% BSA). When incubated with Ab2 (10 μg/mL) under stirring for 2.5 h, C-dots@H-MnO2@Ab2 was collected by centrifugation and redispersed in PBS at 4 °C for further use.

Scheme 1B shows the procedure of dual-signal DBP immunoassay. The coating antigens dissolved in the coating buffer were first dropped onto the 96-well microliter plate. After overnight incubation at 4 °C, the wells were washed 3 times with PBST. Then each well was supplemented with 150 μL of blocking buffer to block the nonspecific active sites for 45 min at 37 °C, followed by addition of 50 μL of standard solutions or samples and 50 μL of Ab1 into each well. After 1 h of incubation and a washing step, 100 μL of C-dots@H-MnO2@Ab2 was added into each well and kept for 1 h at 37 °C. Finally, the solution was incubated for 1 min upon the addition of 25 μL of H2O2 (1.6 mM) for fluorescence measurements. Afterwards, 50 μL of TMB (0.1 mg/mL) was added and incubated for 1 min under light irradiation (365 nm) for absorbance measurements.

Measurements were carried out in triplicate, the mean values of F/F0 and A/A0 were plotted against the logarithm of the concentration of analyte to obtain respective standard curves. Where F and A are the fluorescence and colorimetric signals with DBP, F0 and A0 are the signals with the absence of DBP. The limit of detection (LOD, based on 90% inhibition, IC10) (Zhu et al., 2021; Adrian et al., 2009) and the detection range (IC20-IC80) were obtained by interpolating from a four-parameter logistic equation fit to the sigmoidal curves using Origin 9.0 software.

ce:section.ce:section-title: DBP determination of environmental water and beverage samples
ce:section.ce:para: Environmental water samples were collected at 25 sites of 6 water bodies (Yunliang River, Yangtze River, Grand Canal, Beijing-Hangzhou Canal, Jinshan Hill Scenic Area, and Jiaoshan Hill Scenic Area) in the main city of Zhenjiang, Jiangsu Province. The latitude and longitude information of sampling sites was listed in Table S2. In addition, a total of 119 beverage samples purchased from local markets, were grouped into 7 categories, as mineral water (n = 25), carbonated drinks (n = 10), juice drinks (n = 6), tea drinks (n = 16), protein drinks (n = 36), coffee (n = 12), and alcoholic beverages (n = 14). The beverages were produced in Beijing, Jiangsu, Anhui, Zhejiang, Henan, Heilongjiang, Guizhou, Sichuan, Shandong, Shanghai, Hubei, Fujian and Guangdong, all in China. Further detailed information of beverage samples was shown in Table S3.

All samples were filtered to remove particles larger than 0.22 µm and adjusted to pH 6.5 before determination. Furthermore, for the carbonated drinks, each sample was ultrasonically degassed for 10 min. For protein drinks, tea drinks and coffee, 2 mL of ethanol was added into the samples (10 mL), then shook violently for 5 min, followed by centrifugation (10,000 rpm, 4 °C) for 10 min. The upper fat layer was completely removed while the aqueous layer in the middle part was transferred into a clean glass tube for analysis (Zhang et al., 2011).

The pretreated environmental water and beverage samples were used to determine DBP by the established dual-signal immunoassay, and the concentration of DBP was displayed with the result of fluorescence analysis. Concentrations below the limit of quantification (LOQ, IC20) were assigned a value of zero for statistical analysis (Guo et al., 2012).

To verify the reliability of this proposed method, DBP in environmental water and beverage samples were also measured using LC-MS/MS according to previously reported methods (Chang et al., 2014). The details of LC-MS/MS analysis were presented in the Supporting Information.

Building on the Technical Guidance Document (TGD) on risk assessment recommended by the European Commission (EC, 2003) and previous studies (Liu et al., 2020; Zhang et al., 2013, 2020b), the risk quotient (RQ) method was employed to evaluate the potential ecological risks of DBP. The calculation method of RQ followed Eq. (1).
(1)
RQ=
MEC
PNEC
Where MEC is the measured environmental concentration of DBP. PNEC is the predicted no effect concentration, which was acquired by the ratio of the long-term no observed effect concentration (NOEC) to the assessment factor (AF) of 10. The chronic toxicity data of DBP to three types of model aquatic organisms obtained from the United States Environmental Protection Agency ECOTOX database (https://cfpub.epa.gov/ecotox/) were summarized in Table 1. The ecological risks were divided into three ranks as low, medium, and high risk with an RQ value of < 0.01, between 0.01 and 1, and > 1, respectively.

To assess the health risk of DBP in beverages, the daily dietary intake was estimated first by the Eq. (2) (Chang et al., 2014; Guo et al., 2012).
(2)
EDI=
CQ
bw
r
uptake
Where EDI (μg/kg-bw/d) is the estimated daily intake from drinking beverages, C (μg /L) is the DBP concentration in beverages, Q (mL/day) is the average daily intake reported in published literature (Wang et al., 2020; Gong et al., 2018), r is the gastrointestinal uptake factor and bw (kg) is the body weight. In this study, ruptake of 100% and an average bw of 60 kg were used for local adults, while the Q values for mineral water, carbonated drinks, juice drinks, tea drinks, protein drinks, coffee, and alcoholic beverage were 56, 35, 26, 72, 26, 22, and 17 mL/day, respectively. The hazard index (HI) is obtained from dividing total EDI by TDI as Eq. (3).
(3)
HI=
∑
i
n
EDI
TDI
Where TDI is the tolerable daily intake toward DBP of 10 μg/kg-bw/d proposed by the European Food Safety Authority (EFSA, 2005). By definition, the HI < 1 indicates that no harm would result from DBP exposure. However, HI > 1 suggests that adverse health effects may occur.

TEM and SEM were first performed to characterize the prepared SiO2, SiO2@MnO2 and H-MnO2. The images revealed that the SiO2 nanoparticles presented a uniform spherical structure ( Fig. 1A and B) with a diameter of 220 nm, while the SiO2@MnO2 showed a clear core-shell structure produced by the deposition of MnO2 on the surface of SiO2 nanoparticles (Fig. 1D and E). After the dissolution of SiO2 inside SiO2@MnO2, TEM (Fig. 1G) and SEM (Fig. 1H) images clearly displayed the hollow spherical structure of H-MnO2 without SiO2 nanoparticle inside.

The successful synthesis of SiO2, SiO2@MnO2 and H-MnO2 was further confirmed by elemental composition and valence analysis using XPS (Fig. 1C, F and I). As shown in Fig. 1F, the characteristic peaks of Mn 2p and Si 2p were clearly observed in the full XPS spectrum of SiO2@MnO2. In Fig. 1I, the characteristic peak of Si 2p has almost disappeared, which evidencing the dissolution of SiO2. Notably, the characteristic peaks at 654.1 (Mn 2p 1/2) and 642.2 eV (Mn 2p 3/2) ( Fig. 2A) was corresponding to 11.9 eV spin-induced energy separation for Mn4+ 2p electrons (Shang et al., 2016), while the characteristic peaks of Si 2p (Fig. 2B) shifted from 102.7 to 101.9 eV, showing that the participation of Mn has an effect on the binding energy of Si element (Jiang et al., 2020). Furthermore, EDS was employed to acquire further insight into the chemical composition of the synthesized nanomaterials (Fig. S1). It should be noted that the amount of Si signal in the spectrum of H-MnO2 weakened and almost disappeared, which was consistent with the results of XPS analysis. Additionally, the EDS elemental mapping indicated each element was homogeneously distributed throughout the synthesized nanomaterials (Fig. S2).

The UV-Vis absorption and fluorescence spectra of prepared C-dots were shown in Fig. 2C. The absorption peak at 342 nm and fluorescence emission peak at 450 nm were consistent with previous literature (J. Zhang et al., 2019). Besides, the XPS spectrum for C-dots indicated the presence of C, N, and O elements (Fig. 2D). As shown in Fig. 2E, the liquid dispersion of C-dots appeared claybank color under sunlight while emit clear cyan fluorescence under ultraviolet light. Thereafter, the morphology of as-prepared C-dots was characterized by high-resolution TEM (HRTEM) ( Fig. 3A and B). The clear lattice spacings around 0.34 nm were observed which corresponds to graphite 002 lattice spacing (Fig. 3B) (Doustkhah et al., 2021b; Hassandoost et al., 2019). Interestingly, the broad diffraction peaks corresponding to graphite 002 and 100 were observed in the XRD spectrum (Fig. 3E) (Martindale et al., 2015; Gong et al., 2014), which agreed well with the TEM results.

As shown in Fig. 3C, the loading of C-dots did not change the shape of H-MnO2, while the lattice distance (0.34 nm) belonging to C-dots was observed in Fig. 3D. Moreover, the C-dots@H-MnO2 had the same diffraction peaks as H-MnO2 and these characteristic peak positions matched the crystal structure of α-MnO2 (Zhao et al., 2019; Leong and Yang, 2019). Interestingly, C-dots@H-MnO2 exhibited a broad diffraction peak at around 42.1°, which may be attributed to the (100) plane of C-dots (Fig. 3F). Collectively, these results demonstrated that C-dots were successfully encapsulated into H-MnO2. As soon as the positively charged PEI was coated on C-dots@H-MnO2, the zeta potential dramatically increased from − 9.66 to 11.8 mV ( Fig. 4A), while the hydrodynamic size changed from 190.5 to 214.2 nm (Fig. 4B). After conjugation with Ab2, the C-dots@H-MnO2@Ab2 had zeta potential of 27.9 mV and particle diameter of 234.0 nm. Finally, the stability of the prepared C-dots@H-MnO2@Ab2 was determined. As shown in Fig. S3, the reserving rate of fluorometric and colorimetric signals were 89.8% and 92.1%, respectively (store at 4 °C for 20 days), showing that the prepared material has a satisfactory stability.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Mechanism of dual-signal immunoassay for DBP detection
ce:section.ce:para: Scheme 1 depicted the protocol of this dual-signal immunoassay which applied C-dots@H-MnO2@Ab2 as a tracer antibody for producing fluorometric and colorimetric signals. The presence of DBP in the sample solution initially competed with the immobilized coating antigen to bind with Ab1, and the tracer antibody subsequently binds with Ab1 specifically. High DBP concentration thus caused the decrease of C-dots@H-MnO2@Ab2 complex in the microplate. Upon the competitive immunoreaction, H2O2 was added into the microplate well to dissolve H-MnO2 for releasing C-dots and Mn (II), that could induce intensive fluorescent emission and enhanced catalysis of C-dots for TMB oxidation.

Specifically, the fluorescence of C-dots was quenched by H-MnO2 via fluorescence resonance energy transfer processes due to a favorable overlap (Fang et al., 2016; Qiu et al., 2021) between the fluorescence emission spectra of C-dots and the absorption bands of H-MnO2 ( Fig. 5A). While H2O2 could accelerate the degradation of H-MnO2 under an acid environment to release C-dots and abundant free Mn2+ through the following reactions (Pan et al., 2019):

↑

The fluorescence of C-dots can be restored after the degradation of H-MnO2 which blocked the fluorescence resonance energy transfer process between C-dots and H-MnO2. As shown in Fig. 5B, the C-dots@MnO2 displayed a greatly improved fluorescent signal under pH 6 compared with pH 7, particularly in the presence of H2O2, which could accelerate the decomposition of MnO2. In addition, the C-dots as a photo-oxidase nanozyme could produce singlet oxygen (1O2) upon light irradiation, then Mn (II) was oxidized to Mn (III) by 1O2 to achieve its role as a catalytic mediator enhancing the oxidation of TMB (J. Zhang et al., 2019). The catalysis of C-dots was also studied under different conditions. As shown in Fig. 5C, light irradiation (365 nm) alone cannot oxidize TMB, while in the presence of C-dots, a slight color change appeared for TMB. Meanwhile, some undecomposed H-MnO2 could promote the oxidation of TMB due to its enzyme-like activities (Wu et al., 2021; Ma et al., 2020). However, the strongest absorption peak at 652 nm (responsible for the observed blue color of the TMB oxidation product) appeared with C-dots@H-MnO2 and light together, as the catalysis of C-dots/Mn (II) system was activated by light.

The detection conditions including the reaction time, pH value and concentrations of antigen and antibody were optimized to deliver a high sensitivity toward DBP analysis. Fig. S4A and S4B revealed that both fluorescence intensity and absorbance reached maximum at pH 6.5 probably because an acidic environment promoted the decomposition of H-MnO2, but strong acidic or alkali environments could affect the specific binding between antibody and antigen. To ensure the complete decomposition of H-MnO2, the reaction time was optimized. As displayed in Fig. S4C and S4D, the fluorescence intensity and absorbance values were stable from 1 to 8 min after adding H2O2, meaning that H-MnO2 was entirely decomposed in one minute. Furthermore, the optimal concentrations of antigen (Fig. S4E and S4F) and antibody (Fig. S4G and S4H) were determined to be 0.6 and 1.0 μg/L, respectively. Therefore, the pH, the reaction time, the concentrations of antigen and antibody were selected to be 6.5, 1 min, 0.6 and 1.0 μg/L.

Under optimal conditions, the analytical performance of this proposed method was evaluated. The absorbance values at 652 nm decreased with the increase of DBP concentration. A standard curve was obtained in the detection range from 1.31 to 93.1 µg/L with a limit of detection of 0.692 μg/L ( Fig. 6A). Meanwhile, the fluorescent assay provided a higher sensitivity for DBP detection with LOD of 0.243 μg/L and detection range of 1.00–145 μg/L (Fig. 6B). By contrast, the detection limits of this proposed fluorescent and colorimetric methods were approximately 13-fold and 5-fold lower than that of the conventional ELISA with the same antibody, respectively (Fig. S5).

The accuracy of the established method was evaluated by analyzing beverage and water samples spiked with DBP at different concentrations. Table S4 shows that good recoveries (79.6–118.5%) with an intraassay coefficient of variation (CV) from 2.5% to 8.6% were obtained. Moreover, the reliability was further determined by instrument methods (Table S5). Paired-samples T-test proved that the difference between the analysis results of the two groups was not statistically significant (t = 0.88, P > 0.05). All the above assessment demonstrated that the established approach was accurate and reliable for DBP determination for environmental water and beverage samples.

Meanwhile, Table S6 indicates that the developed dual-signal immunoassay had some advantages over other reported methods including the low detection limit, high throughput and convenience for DBP detection. For instance, sample preparation steps generally account for over 80% of the whole analysis time of instrumental analytical techniques (Odoardi et al., 2014). It is complex and time-consuming relative to the limited procedures of sample pretreatment in our proposed method. Besides, the analysis time for LC-MS/MS and GC-MS after pretreatment was about 20 min and 28 min for each sample, respectively (Wang et al., 2021; Chang et al., 2014). This is in striking contrast to our developed high-throughput immunoassay, which can complete the determination of hundreds of samples within 4 h after coating step.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Investigating the occurrences and ecological risk of DBP in environmental waters
ce:section.ce:para: The established method was applied to evaluate the occurrences and therefore the ecological risk of DBP in the surface water of Zhenjiang City. As displayed in Table S7, DBP was found in all sampling sites with concentrations ranging from 16.5 to 32.1 μg/L. The concentrations of DBP in these sites were higher than the limited value of 3 μg/L regulated under China’s standard GB 3838-2002 “Environmental quality standards of surface water” (Zhang et al., 2017). It evidently illustrates that DBP was a common and broadly distributed contaminant in surface waters at the studied region. Similarly, DBP was widely distributed in aquatic environments around the world which can be seen from Table S8. The DBP concentrations in surface waters of Zhenjiang were paralleled to Wuhan section of the Yangtze River but much higher than others. In addition, the DBP concentrations of most sampling sites detected in 2020 were lower than those measured in 2016 ( Fig. 7). Notably, the DBP levels in Jinshan Hill Scenic Area and Jiaoshan Hill Scenic Area had increased almost threefold, which might be related to frequent human activities. As shown in Fig. 8, the mean concentration of the 13 sampling sites, which sampled in both 2016 and 2020, had decreased to 22.9 μg/L from 43.5 μg/L with a significant difference (P < 0.01, ANOVA). It was potentially due to the reasonable and effective management by the local government.

Based on the results of pollution investigation, the potential ecological risks of DBP in these aquatic areas were subsequently assessed. As shown in Table 1, the RQ values of DBP in six aquatic areas ranged from 1.85 to 3.15 (the highest values of RQ in each aquatic area). For individual species, fish suffered the most serious risk from DBP (RQ > 1 at all aquatic areas). In terms of sampling sites, the RQ of DBP exhibited the highest value at the Jinshan Scenic Spot while the lowest value at Yunliang River. At Jinshan Scenic Spot, the RQ values for algae, crustacean, and fish were bigger than 1, suggesting that DBP posted a high risk for these three aquatic sensitive model species. By comparison, DBP only posed a high risk to fish at Yunliang River.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Evaluating the occurrences and health risk of DBP in beverages
ce:section.ce:para: The proposed immunoassay was also used to investigate the occurrences and further the health risk of DBP in beverages. As shown in Table 2, the concentration of DBP in diverse beverages ranged from 0 to 553 μg/L, with the detection frequency varying from 20% to 83.3%. Among different beverages, the detection frequency and mean concentration of DBP varied greatly. The highest DBP concentration (mean: 158 μg/L) was detected in alcoholic beverages, while the lowest concentration (mean: 0.831 μg/L) occurred in mineral water. That might be because DBP has low solubility in water and migrates more easily into products with a high ethanol content (Chatonnet et al., 2014). Moreover, the highest and lowest detection frequency appeared in protein drinks and mineral water respectively. In an overall perspective, DBP was found in 65.5% of the beverages analyzed with a mean concentration of 52.4 μg/L. The specific migration limit for DBP from food packaging materials to food is 300 μg/kg (Yang et al., 2019). There were only individual protein drinks and alcoholic beverages which contained DBP exceeding the value. Compared to the results we detected in 2016, the mean concentration in coffee dropped above 20 times, but increased in protein drinks and alcoholic beverages. As far as the three types of beverages were concerned, the mean concentration of DBP detected in 2020 were paralleled to that detected 2016, which can be seen in Fig. 8 (P > 0.05, ANOVA).

Table S9 showed that DBP was frequently detected in different beverages worldwide with the detection frequency ranging from 23% to 100%, similar to this study. Furthermore, the concentration of DBP in alcoholic beverages were far higher than others. For example, in France, the DBP concentration of 72.8 μg/L in red wine was at least 33-fold higher than other beverages (Cariou et al., 2016). Meanwhile, it can be seen that the concentrations of DBP in different beverages, even the same beverage but from different countries, varied greatly due to different production region, product characteristics, and packaging (Wu et al., 2019).

The calculated EDI values were presented in Table 3. Among the 7 types of beverages, the EDI (based on the mean concentration of DBP) of tea drinks was higher than others, revealing that the tea drinks contributed most to health risk, apparently due to the highest consumption frequency (Q = 72 mL/day) in local area. Alcohol beverage was the second largest contributor, which feasibly caused by the highest DBP concentration. The total EDI value calculated based on mean concentration of DBP was 0.188 μg/kg-bw/d, with EDI corresponding to the median concentration was 0.0785 μg/kg-bw/d. Compared with the TDI (10 μg/kg-bw/d), the exposure to DBP by drinking beverages was sufficiently low, even when the highest concentration of DBP was used to calculate EDI. The values of HI for health risks which are lower than 1.0 (Table 3), indicating that exposure to DBP through beverages was tolerable and safe for local residents.

In summary, we developed a novel dual-signal immunoassay for DBP detection. Compared with LC-MS/MS which was commonly used in the detection of DBP, the method developed in this study had considerable sensitivity and offered many advantages such as low cost and high throughput, especially its suitability for the analysis of a large number of samples in a short time. Using our method, a large number of environmental water and beverage samples were analyzed, further the ecological and health risks of DBP were evaluated. Our results showed that the pollution of DBP in most water bodies has been improved, and the content of DBP in coffee had been significantly reduced, compared with the data of 2016. Furthermore, DBP in the water bodies still posed a serious ecological risk to the surrounding environment even though its level reduced compared to 2016, whereas the health risk caused by DBP in beverages was tolerable.
